stories.headline: UN says families are fleeing Beirut as Lebanon's war toll passes 3,400 dead and hunger spreads
stories.summary: UN agencies reported on June 2 that families are fleeing Beirut amid Israeli threats of renewed strikes on Hezbollah, with Lebanese health authorities counting more than 3,400 people killed and over 10,000 injured since fighting escalated on March 2. More than a million people have been displaced, and food-security experts say roughly 1.24 million — nearly a quarter of the population — lack adequate food.

perspectives.keyFraming: A deepening humanitarian emergency — mass displacement from Beirut, 3,400-plus dead and widespread hunger — beneath the disputed ceasefire claims.
perspectives.summary: UN News documents civilians fleeing Beirut under threat of renewed Israeli strikes, with cumulative tolls since March 2 reaching 3,412 dead and more than 10,000 injured, over a million displaced and 1.24 million facing inadequate food. By June 3, AFP reported fresh strikes on the south killed five, including a child, and wounded 48, damaging a government hospital — deepening the civilian emergency beneath competing claims about whether a truce is holding.

sources.title: Lebanon says Israeli strikes on south kill 5, wound hospital staff

stories.headline: US and Iran trade strikes across the Gulf; Iranian attack kills one and damages Kuwait's main airport
stories.summary: In the most intense exchange since the April ceasefire, the US struck an Iranian military ground-control station on Qeshm Island near the Strait of Hormuz. Iran fired missiles and drones at Bahrain and Kuwait, hitting Kuwait International Airport — killing one and injuring more than 60. Kuwait suspended flights and expelled two Iranian diplomats; Tehran said it had stopped communicating with ceasefire mediators.

perspectives.keyFraming: A fragile, weeks-old ceasefire is buckling as both sides trade direct strikes while mediators stall and a civilian airport is hit.
perspectives.summary: US forces struck an Iranian ground-control station on Qeshm Island in response to Iranian attacks on Bahrain and Kuwait. Kuwait's main airport was heavily damaged, one person killed and dozens injured, prompting a flight suspension. Iranian agencies said Tehran had halted contact with ceasefire mediators, while Trump maintained that talks remained ongoing despite the violence.

sources.title: Iran strikes Kuwait's main airport and kills 1 as ceasefire is tested again
Iran and the US trade strikes in the Persian Gulf, further testing the ceasefire

perspectives.keyFraming: Gulf states bear the human cost: Kuwait, a non-combatant, sees its civilian airport hit and expels Iranian diplomats.
perspectives.summary: Kuwait's Foreign Ministry said an Iranian drone-and-missile strike on its international airport killed one person and injured more than 60, with some suffering amputations. Kuwait declared two Iranian embassy staff persona non grata. Iran claimed it had targeted the US Fifth Fleet headquarters and a regional airbase in retaliation for the Qeshm strikes; Bahrain said it intercepted three missiles and drones.

sources.title: Kuwait's Foreign Ministry says one killed in Iranian attack on airport
Iran war live: No progress on negotiations with the US – Araghchi

perspectives.keyFraming: State-aligned coverage casts Iran's strikes as legitimate retaliation for the US attack on Qeshm and reports the Gulf diplomatic fallout factually.
perspectives.summary: Chinese state broadcaster CGTN reported Kuwait summoning the Iranian envoy and expelling two diplomats amid the Iranian attacks, framing the rupture within a broader narrative of a fragile US-Iran ceasefire under strain. Iranian and state-aligned outlets cast the cross-Gulf exchange as a response to American aggression near the Strait of Hormuz, with Tehran saying it struck US naval and air assets in the region.

sources.title: Kuwait summons Iranian envoy, expels 2 diplomats amid Iranian attacks

stories.headline: Ukrainian long-range drones hit St. Petersburg oil terminal and naval base as Russia's flagship economic forum opens
stories.summary: Overnight into June 3, Ukrainian drones flew about 1,000 km to strike the Petersburg Oil Terminal — setting it ablaze — and facilities at the Kronstadt naval base, hours before the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum opened under Putin's patronage. Ukraine claimed it hit the Baltic Fleet corvette Boikiy. Russia's Defense Ministry said air defenses downed 354 drones over 16 regions, and the Investigative Committee opened a 'terrorism' probe.

perspectives.keyFraming: A symbolic deep strike timed to embarrass Putin's prestige forum, hitting energy and naval targets hundreds of miles inside Russia.
perspectives.summary: Ukraine struck a major St. Petersburg oil terminal and the Kronstadt naval base hours before the economic forum opened. Zelensky said drones flew more than 1,000 km and also hit a weapons plant in the Tambov region. Russia reported intercepting 354 drones overnight, but a large fire broke out at the terminal. The attack underscored the widening reach of Ukraine's long-range drone campaign.

sources.title: Ukrainian drones strike a St. Petersburg oil terminal ahead of Putin visit
Russian warship at Baltic Fleet base struck overnight, Ukraine says

perspectives.keyFraming: The strike spotlights the war's intrusion into Russia's economic showcase as air defenses over one of its most fortified cities appear overwhelmed.
perspectives.summary: Independent Russian-exile and European outlets framed the attack as the forum opening 'under the shadow of' Ukrainian strikes. The strike hit energy infrastructure and the Baltic Fleet's Kronstadt base, with Ukraine claiming the corvette Boikiy — used to escort Russia's sanctioned 'shadow fleet' of tankers.

sources.title: St. Petersburg Economic Forum Opens Under Shadow of Ukrainian Drone Strikes

perspectives.keyFraming: Russia frames the attack as a repelled 'terrorist' drone raid, emphasizing hundreds downed and the forum proceeding as planned.
perspectives.summary: TASS reported Ukrainian UAVs attacking infrastructure in Kronstadt and the Kirovsky and Krasnoselsky districts, injuring several people, while air defenses destroyed 354 drones over 16 regions and the Sea of Azov. The Investigative Committee classified the incidents as terrorist attacks, and state coverage stressed the forum proceeding with Putin's plenary set for June 5.

sources.title: Ukrainian drones attack infrastructure in St. Petersburg, injuring several people

stories.headline: Israeli strikes kill nine in Lebanon and reach Beirut outskirts as US-mediated talks enter a second day
stories.summary: Israeli strikes across Lebanon on June 3 killed at least nine, including two medics in Chehour and six near Tyre, and hit at least 10 vehicles — one an ambulance — reaching a few kilometers south of Beirut. Israel's military chief said there would be 'no ceasefire for our forces.' The strikes came as the fourth round of Israel-Lebanon talks continued in Washington, where Rubio voiced hope for a joint security statement 'independent from Hezbollah.'

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel keeps killing Lebanese civilians — including medics and an ambulance crew — while negotiating, exposing the gap between de-escalation claims and the ground.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera reported Israeli attacks killed nine, struck an ambulance and a Lebanese soldier, and reached Beirut's outskirts despite claims that Netanyahu had agreed to cancel Beirut strikes. Lebanon's Health Ministry detailed medic deaths in Chehour and six killed near Tyre. The strikes coincided with the second day of the Washington talks.

sources.title: Israeli attacks kill nine in Lebanon, reach Beirut outskirts

stories.headline: Israeli fire kills several in Gaza as Israel's top court orders Red Cross access to Palestinian detainees
stories.summary: Gaza's Health Ministry reported several Palestinians killed and dozens wounded over the past day amid a fraying truce; the Israeli military said it killed operatives who threatened troops and a Hamas member tied to the October 7 abductions. Separately, Israel's High Court of Justice unanimously annulled the government's blanket ban on Red Cross visits to Palestinian security detainees, finding no legal basis for it.

perspectives.keyFraming: Israeli legal accountability: the High Court strikes down a blanket Red Cross ban even as the military touts killing an October 7 kidnapper.
perspectives.summary: Israeli outlets reported the High Court annulled the policy denying ICRC access to Palestinian security detainees, ruling the state lacked a legal foundation. The military said it killed a Hamas cell commander involved in the October 7 abductions and operatives threatening troops, with Gaza's toll rising amid the strained truce.

sources.title: IDF kills Oct. 7 terrorist who kidnapped Hersh Goldberg-Polin, Eliya Cohen, Alon Ohel

perspectives.keyFraming: Coverage centers the continued Palestinian death toll under a truce that is fraying in practice rather than the Israeli legal or military framing.
perspectives.summary: Regional and Israeli liberal coverage reported Israeli fire killing several Palestinians and wounding dozens over the past day in Gaza, situating the casualties within a ceasefire that is breaking down on the ground even as both sides claim to observe it.

sources.title: Israeli Fire in Gaza Kills Five, Wounds 36 Over Past Day, Reports Say

stories.headline: US strikes Iran's Qeshm Island as Iranian missiles and drones hit Kuwait airport and target Bahrain
stories.summary: US Central Command struck an Iranian military ground-control station on Qeshm Island in the Strait of Hormuz on June 3 after Iran launched ballistic missiles and drones at Gulf targets. Missiles aimed at Kuwait fell short and three fired at Bahrain were intercepted, but a drone hit Kuwait's main airport, killing at least one person (an Indian national) and injuring dozens. The exchange followed reports that ceasefire-extension talks had stalled.

perspectives.keyFraming: Tit-for-tat strikes mark a sharp escalation as ceasefire diplomacy appears to falter.
perspectives.summary: Iran's Revolutionary Guards said they targeted the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain and other US facilities; the US said it intercepted most missiles and struck Qeshm Island in response. A drone hitting Kuwait's main airport caused civilian casualties as reports indicated that talks to extend a ceasefire had broken down.

sources.title: Iran fires missiles and US strikes Iran facility after reports of faltering peace talks

perspectives.keyFraming: Civilian toll mounts as an Iranian drone hits Kuwait's main airport, killing a foreign worker.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera reported a drone struck Kuwait's main airport, killing an Indian citizen and injuring more than 60 people, while Tehran said the US had attacked Qeshm Island. Coverage centered the civilian impact and the risk to Gulf aviation and shipping as both sides claimed strikes on the other.

sources.title: Iranian drone attack kills Indian citizen in Kuwait after US strikes Qeshm
Iran war updates: No progress on negotiations with the US — Araghchi

perspectives.keyFraming: US forces shoot down Iranian missiles and drones in defense of Gulf allies.
perspectives.summary: Fox News emphasized US and Bahraini air defenses intercepting Iranian missiles aimed at Bahrain and the US military response on Qeshm, framing the strikes as protecting American forces and Gulf partners against Iranian aggression after diplomacy stalled.

sources.title: US forces shoot down Iranian missiles, drones in response to fresh attacks on Kuwait, Bahrain

perspectives.keyFraming: Explainer angle centers Gulf states' exposure and the stakes for Hormuz negotiations.
perspectives.summary: Gulf News laid out what triggered the escalation, the drone strike on Kuwait's airport, the Qeshm Island clashes and the fate of Hormuz talks, stressing the regional stakes for Gulf monarchies caught between Washington and Tehran as energy and aviation links came under threat.

sources.title: US-Iran Escalation Explained: Drone Strike on Kuwait Airport, Qeshm Island Clashes and Fate of Hormuz Talks

stories.headline: Ukrainian drones strike St. Petersburg oil terminal hours before Russia's economic forum opens
stories.summary: Ukrainian long-range drones flew more than 1,000 km to strike one of northwest Russia's largest oil transshipment terminals in St. Petersburg overnight, wounding several people, hours before the SPIEF economic forum opened in the city. Ukraine said it also hit a Russian corvette near Kronstadt. The strikes followed a Russian barrage on Kyiv and Dnipro on June 2 that killed at least 23.

perspectives.keyFraming: A long-range strike embarrasses Moscow as it courts investors at its flagship forum.
perspectives.summary: CBC and NPR reported a fire at the St. Petersburg oil terminal and the timing just hours before Russia's flagship economic forum opened, situating the attack within Ukraine's escalating deep-strike campaign against Russian energy infrastructure far from the front line.

sources.title: Ukrainian drones hit St. Petersburg oil terminal before city hosts Russian economic forum
Ukrainian drones strike a St. Petersburg oil terminal ahead of Putin visit

perspectives.keyFraming: A strike on 'Putin's Davos' city signals Ukraine's deepening long-range reach.
perspectives.summary: CNN reported Zelensky citing 'positive results' from long-range strikes on key targets including the St. Petersburg terminal and a claimed corvette hit near Kronstadt, underscoring Kyiv's intent to bring the war to the city hosting Russia's economic showcase.

sources.title: Ukrainian drones strike St. Petersburg, hours before 'Putin's Davos' opens

perspectives.keyFraming: An escalation cycle: the Ukrainian strike follows a deadly Russian barrage on Kyiv and Dnipro.
perspectives.summary: The Washington Post tied the St. Petersburg strike to the wider exchange of fire, including the June 2 Russian attack on Kyiv and Dnipro that killed at least 23 people, framing the forum's opening against a backdrop of intensifying mutual strikes deep in each country.

sources.title: Ukraine hits oil refinery as Russia opens St. Petersburg economic forum

stories.headline: Trump announces fresh Israel-Lebanon ceasefire as IDF chief says forces will keep operating
stories.summary: President Trump announced a fresh full Israel-Lebanon ceasefire, with Israel agreeing to postpone planned Beirut strikes, conditioned on steps by Hezbollah; an earlier June 1 truce had Israel commit not to strike Beirut's southern suburbs. IDF Chief of Staff Eyal Zamir said 'there is no ceasefire for our forces' and that the military would keep operating in southern Lebanon. The cumulative 2026 Lebanon war toll has exceeded 3,000.

perspectives.keyFraming: A fragile truce: an agreement is reached but the IDF signals it will keep operating.
perspectives.summary: Axios reported a conditional full ceasefire brokered by the US, contingent on Hezbollah taking specified steps, with Israel postponing planned strikes on Beirut even as questions remained over enforcement and the durability of the arrangement.

sources.title: Israel and Lebanon agree to full ceasefire, conditioned on steps by Hezbollah

perspectives.keyFraming: The IDF carves out 'freedom of action' despite the truce, amid Trump-Netanyahu friction.
perspectives.summary: Times of Israel reported Zamir's 'no ceasefire for our forces' line and Netanyahu's defense of a heated call with Trump, signaling the military intends to keep striking perceived threats in southern Lebanon despite the announced agreement to hold fire.

sources.title: Trump announces fresh Lebanon truce as Netanyahu appears to call off Beirut strikes
June 3 liveblog: IDF chief says 'no ceasefire for our forces' in Lebanon

stories.headline: Hezbollah rejects US-brokered Israel-Lebanon ceasefire as 'a farce'; Israeli strikes continue
stories.summary: Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem rejected the US-brokered ceasefire that Israel and Lebanon had agreed, calling it 'a farce' and 'a roadmap to annihilate part of the Lebanese people' and demanding full Israeli withdrawal first. Lebanese President Joseph Aoun urged acceptance, calling the deal a 'last chance.' Israeli strikes continued through June 5, with fresh deaths reported; Lebanon's health ministry says 3,526 have been killed since March 2.

perspectives.keyFraming: Both governments agreed; the militia that does the fighting did not, leaving the deal dead on arrival.
perspectives.summary: Centrist reporting frames Hezbollah's rejection as imperiling the US-brokered deal, which would create 'pilot zones' south of the Litani under exclusive Lebanese Army control in exchange for an IDF withdrawal. The sticking point is sequencing — whether Israel withdraws before Hezbollah pulls fighters from the south — as both sides exchange fresh fire.

sources.title: Israel and Hezbollah Trade Fresh Strikes as Militant Group Rejects Cease-Fire Plan
Israel, Lebanon agree to full ceasefire, but Hezbollah rejects it

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel says it will keep operating 'as planned' and has threatened Beirut if rocket fire on its cities continues.
perspectives.summary: Israeli officials framed the deal as conditional on Hezbollah disarming south of the Litani, with continued strikes on June 4-5 and warnings of action against Beirut. Trump claimed progress on the Lebanon track and said Hezbollah had not formally rejected the offer.

sources.title: June 4: Trump says progress on Lebanon ceasefire, claims Hezbollah did not reject offer

perspectives.keyFraming: A ceasefire that demands Hezbollah retreat under fire is cast as surrender; Arab coverage centers continued Lebanese deaths.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera framed continued Israeli strikes — with deaths and fresh evacuation orders on June 4-5 — as undermining the deal, while Qassem cast a withdrawal-under-attack as capitulation. Coverage emphasizes the civilian toll and Lebanon's demand for a full Israeli pullout before any Hezbollah pullback.

sources.title: Iran war day 98: Tehran raises doubts on deal as Lebanon fighting continues

stories.headline: Putin tells Trump Russia will respond to Ukraine's deep strike on bombers as peace talks stall
stories.summary: After a roughly 75-minute phone call on June 4, President Trump said the conversation would not bring 'immediate peace' and that Vladimir Putin vowed to retaliate for Ukraine's drone attack that reportedly destroyed dozens of Russian strategic bombers. Putin branded Kyiv 'terrorists' over deadly rail attacks he blamed on Ukraine while calling the June 2 Istanbul round 'useful.' Overnight, Russia struck Odesa and Kharkiv as Ukraine hit targets in Crimea.

perspectives.keyFraming: The call signals no near-term peace as Russia promises to retaliate for the bomber strike.
perspectives.summary: Centrist outlets report Trump's account that Putin will respond to Ukraine's airfield drone attack and that the call would not bring immediate peace, even as the Istanbul track limps on and overnight strikes continue.

sources.title: Trump says he spoke with Putin, and Putin plans to respond to Ukraine drone strike
Trump says Putin told him Russia will respond to Ukrainian drone attack on airfields

perspectives.keyFraming: The peace process is stalling as Russia keeps attacking after the call.
perspectives.summary: Western coverage situates the call within a stalled peace process, noting Russia continued striking Ukrainian cities and Ukraine hit Russian targets in Crimea, with little sign the diplomacy is constraining the fighting.

sources.title: Where Things Stand After Ukrainian Drone Strikes Deep In Russia, Istanbul Talks, And A Trump-Putin Call

perspectives.keyFraming: Moscow frames Kyiv as 'terrorists' and promises a systemic response while pressing ahead with its forum agenda.
perspectives.summary: Russian state media casts the rail and other attacks as Kyiv-regime terrorism demanding a 'systemic' response, while stressing that the St. Petersburg economic forum will proceed despite Ukrainian drone threats.

sources.title: Report to Putin on bus strike, SPIEF agenda: Peskov's statements

stories.headline: Zelensky publishes open letter to Putin proposing front-line ceasefire and a summit; Putin vows only to bolster air defenses
stories.summary: On June 4 Zelensky published an open letter to Putin — his first direct public address to the Russian leader since 2022 — proposing talks from the current front line, a full ceasefire under US monitoring, an 'all-for-all' prisoner exchange, and a neutral-hosted summit. The Kremlin said it would respond later. Speaking June 5, Putin answered recent Ukrainian drone strikes by saying Russia would 'strengthen' its air defenses rather than retaliate directly.

perspectives.keyFraming: Ukraine pivots to a public diplomatic offensive after its deep strikes, reframing Kyiv as the side seeking talks.
perspectives.summary: Zelensky's letter proposes a front-line freeze, US-monitored ceasefire and neutral-host summit, while Moscow stays non-committal. Reporting casts it as a notable shift in posture following Ukraine's damaging long-range drone campaign against Russian airfields.

sources.title: Ukraine's Zelenskyy Calls for Peace Talks With Russia in Letter to Putin
'Enough War': Zelensky Calls Putin to Direct Talks and Monitored Ceasefire

perspectives.keyFraming: Putin answers Ukrainian drones with defense, not threats — for now — even as he commands the SPIEF stage.
perspectives.summary: Western coverage notes Putin acknowledged Ukrainian drones 'break through' and pledged to improve air defense, a comparatively restrained public posture amid his St. Petersburg forum showcase and Kyiv's June 3 strike on a city oil terminal.

sources.title: Putin says Russia will bolster air defenses in response to Ukrainian drone attacks
Putin says Russia will bolster its air defenses against Ukraine's long-range drone strikes

perspectives.keyFraming: Russian state framing centers Putin's authority and the forum's success, treating Ukrainian strikes as containable.
perspectives.summary: State media foregrounds Putin's plenary appearance and economic agenda, downplaying the drone damage and presenting the Kremlin as negotiating from strength rather than responding to Kyiv's overtures.

sources.title: Putin to speak at SPIEF plenary session

stories.headline: Putin rejects Zelensky's offer to meet at SPIEF, calls open letter 'boorish' and sees 'no point' in talks
stories.summary: At the St. Petersburg International Economic Forum on June 5, President Putin publicly rebuffed Zelensky's June 4 open letter proposing a leaders' meeting and full ceasefire, dismissing it as containing 'rude' remarks and saying he sees 'no point' in meeting without a deal ready to sign. He again rejected an immediate ceasefire, insisting on a comprehensive settlement aligned with the 2025 Anchorage understandings reached with Trump.

perspectives.keyFraming: Framed as Putin slamming the door on near-term diplomacy, demanding a finished settlement rather than a leaders' meeting or interim truce.
perspectives.summary: AP-syndicated coverage reported Putin dismissed Zelensky's open letter as containing 'rude' remarks and said a meeting makes no sense without a deal ready to sign, reiterating his rejection of an immediate ceasefire from the SPIEF plenary stage.

sources.title: Putin rejects Zelenskyy's offer to meet, saying he sees 'no point' in it
Putin cites 'rude remarks' in rejecting Zelenskyy's open letter request to meet

perspectives.keyFraming: Russian state framing centers Putin's command of the SPIEF stage and presents Moscow as negotiating from strength, not avoiding peace.
perspectives.summary: State media foregrounded Putin's plenary appearance and economic agenda, presenting his rejection of Zelensky's offer as a principled insistence on a comprehensive settlement rather than a refusal of diplomacy.

sources.title: Putin to speak at SPIEF plenary session
